nativeRange.habitat: Steep seaward reefs over sand and rubble areas; typically encountered at 20 m or deeper along the East African coast and Indian Ocean island groups.

decorPreferences: fine sand bed (5-8 cm) for burrowing and sleeping

diet.notes: Feeds on small benthic crustaceans and zooplankton in the wild. In captivity, has been reported to accept frozen mysis, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped marine meats, and quality pellets/flakes after acclimation; many specimens are initially shy and have been documented as best enticed with live saltwater feeder shrimp or copepods. Frequent small feedings are generally advised. Will tend to hunt pyramidellid snails, fireworms, and small flatworms, which can make it a useful pest-control wrasse.

goodWith: larger ornamental shrimp introduced before the wrasse

imageCandidates.notes: Photographer Tony Rebelo; 933x695. Vibrant terminal-phase male showing the diagnostic orange-red green-scribbled face, yellow-green upper body and bold dark maroon-black lateral band; tack-sharp, whole fish in frame, dark reef substrate makes colours pop.
Photographer Brian Gratwicke; 1024x683. Sharp, well-lit full-body shot with classic yellow-green head, orange snout scribbles and reddish body; clean alternate angle.
Photographer Dieter Karner; 1709x1132. Colourful specimen showing green facial scribbles and red-edged dorsal well; slight motion blur toward the tail keeps it non-recommended.

summary: The Radiant Wrasse is a peaceful, small-bodied Halichoeres from the Western Indian Ocean, appreciated for its vivid adult coloration and its tendency to hunt fireworms and pyramidellid snails. It generally does well in reef tanks but requires a fine sand bed for burrowing and a tightly sealed lid.
careNotes:
The Radiant Wrasse (Halichoeres iridis), also widely sold as the Iridis Wrasse, is native to the Western Indian Ocean, ranging from the East African coast south to Natal, South Africa, and extending across to Madagascar, the Seychelles, and the Chagos Archipelago. In the wild it is typically encountered on steep seaward reefs over sand and rubble, most often at depths of around 20 m or deeper — the recorded depth range spans 6 to 43 m — so wild-caught specimens are frequently collected from the deeper end of the normal reef-fish collection range.

Adult size is small for the genus, with maximum reported lengths converging around 11.4 to 11.5 cm. A tank of at least 300 liters is recommended: Halichoeres are active mid-water swimmers that also burrow at night, and the extra space also accommodates a haremic group.

This species is a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite, meaning individuals are born female and the dominant fish in a group will transition to male over time. It is typically maintained as a single individual, a male-female pair, or a male with several females (a small harem). Multiple males in the same tank tend to be incompatible.

Like most wrasses in this genus, the Radiant Wrasse is a strong jumper, and a tightly-fitting lid with no large gaps is essential.

Reef compatibility is generally good with corals, but ornamental fan worms, very small shrimp, pyramidellid snails, and small flatworms are likely to be eaten — this pest-control tendency is often considered a benefit in reef setups focused on clams or small-polyp stony corals.

Newer specimens can present a moderate feeding challenge, and some individuals may need to be coaxed initially with live foods before accepting frozen or prepared diets.
breedingNotes: Distinct pairing during breeding has been documented in the wild. As a protogynous hermaphrodite, a lone female or a group containing only females can produce a dominant male over time. Captive breeding has not been widely reported.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 11.5 cm TL vs LiveAquaria 11.4 cm - close agreement), temperature range (FishBase preferred 25.1-27.8°C vs LiveAquaria 22.2-25.6°C, averaged to ~23.5-27°C), native range, taxonomy (Labridae, Eupercaria), depth range (6-43 m), and habitat (steep seaward reefs, sand/rubble).

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size (300 L vs LiveAquaria's 50 gal / ~190 L - biased toward the larger value), confirmed peaceful temperament, harem grouping, sand-bed burrowing behavior (~5 cm minimum), jumping risk, and protogynous hermaphroditism.

fish.breedingNotes: The Radiant Wrasse spawns in pairs in open water in the wild, releasing eggs and sperm into the water column (pelagic spawning). Captive breeding has not been widely achieved. As a protogynous hermaphrodite, the dominant individual in a group transitions from female to male; in a haremic group, removing the male can trigger the dominant female to change sex over time.
